reason: The XML parser in the XPath-based message selector evaluation creates a DocumentBuilderFactory without disabling external entity resolution, allowing remote consumers to exploit XXE via crafted XML messages.
hint_l1: Message consumption and selector evaluation logic
hint_l2: An XML external entity (XXE) vulnerability allows remote consumers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving an XPath-based selector when dequeuing XML messages. The XML parser used for selector processing does not disable external entity resolution, allowing attackers to craft malicious XML content that triggers external entity processing.

reason: The Framed struct has an unsound blanket Unpin implementation, allowing it to be moved after pinning while its methods use unsafe Pin::new_unchecked on the inner I/O stream, leading to use-after-free when the inner type relies on pin guarantees.
hint_l1: Asynchronous stream processing and data framing infrastructure
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in an asynchronous I/O framing component due to an unsound safety assertion that allows a pinned wrapper type to be moved freely. The inner I/O stream, which may depend on stable memory placement, can be invalidated when the enclosing type is relocated after pinning. This violates Rust's pin safety guarantees and can lead to memory corruption, arbitrary code execution, or denial of service via a crafted I/O type.

reason: The ecard_recipients POST parameter values are directly concatenated into a SQL IN clause via implode() without type casting or sanitization, allowing an authenticated user to inject arbitrary SQL and compromise the database.
hint_l1: User communication and greeting card features
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where user-supplied recipient identifiers from a POST request are directly concatenated into a SQL query without sanitization or type casting. An authenticated member user can inject arbitrary SQL through the recipient selection parameter, enabling blind condition-based and time-based SQL injection attacks that can exfiltrate, modify, or delete database contents.

reason: The sqlite3 driver's MetaColumns(), metaForeignKeys(), and metaIndexes() methods directly interpolate the user-supplied table name into SQL query strings without parameterization, allowing arbitrary SQL injection.
hint_l1: Database driver layer and schema introspection logic
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where a crafted table name is interpolated directly into SQL query strings used by schema metadata retrieval methods, rather than being passed as a bound parameter. An attacker who can control the table name argument can execute arbitrary SQL statements against the database.

reason: The SimpleParameterList class serializes query parameters into SQL text without properly escaping or quoting the values when using simple query mode, allowing SQL injection via crafted parameter values that exploit line comment generation through negation.
hint_l1: JDBC query execution and parameter handling
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists in the JDBC driver when using a non-default query mode. Parameter values are directly interpolated into SQL statements without proper escaping or quoting, allowing an attacker to break out of the intended SQL context and inject arbitrary SQL commands via specially crafted input values.

reason: The change request title is rendered without escaping in the UI template, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary scripts via a crafted title that executes in other users' browsers.
hint_l1: Change request rendering and UI template layer
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability allows a user without any specific rights to perform script injection and remote code execution by inserting a crafted title when creating a new change request. The title value is rendered without proper output escaping in the user interface, allowing arbitrary script execution in the context of other users' sessions.

reason: The UeAuthPostRequestProcedure function performs interface type assertions on values retrieved from internal context maps without nil checks, causing a panic when a crafted authentication request references non-existent subscriber identity mappings.
hint_l1: SBI request processing and subscriber context management
hint_l2: A nil interface conversion vulnerability exists in the authentication service's request handling logic. When processing an authentication request, the code attempts to perform a type assertion on an interface value without first verifying it is non-nil. A remote attacker can trigger a panic and crash the service by sending a crafted request that causes a lookup for a non-existent internal mapping, resulting in a complete denial of service.

reason: The XML parser used to process remote update feed responses is instantiated without disabling external entity resolution, allowing XXE attacks when an attacker controls or intercepts the update manifest.
hint_l1: Software update checking and configuration parsing
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability exists in the update feed parsing logic. When processing remotely fetched XML update manifests, the XML parser is not configured to disable external entity resolution, allowing an attacker who controls or intercepts the update feed to read arbitrary files or perform server-side request forgery via crafted XML payloads.

reason: The application generates a cryptographic salt using uniqid(), a time-based function with insufficient entropy, allowing an attacker to brute force the salt offline and forge valid encrypted password recovery codes for privilege escalation.
hint_l1: Cryptographic operations and application configuration setup
hint_l2: An insufficient entropy vulnerability exists in the generation of a cryptographic secret used for encryption operations. The secret is derived from a time-based, non-cryptographic function whose output is predictable. An attacker can infer the approximate generation timestamp from publicly accessible information and brute force the remaining entropy offline, allowing them to forge valid encrypted password recovery tokens and escalate privileges to administrator.

reason: The mount stub cleanup routine does not validate that resolved paths remain within the container root, allowing a malicious build frontend to craft symlinks that cause the cleaner to delete arbitrary files on the host filesystem.
hint_l1: Container execution and filesystem mount lifecycle management
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists in the container build process where the cleanup logic that removes temporary files created for mountpoints can be tricked into deleting files outside the container's root directory on the host system. A malicious build frontend or build definition can exploit this to achieve arbitrary host file deletion.

reason: A race condition in cache mount subpath resolution allows parallel build steps sharing the same cache mount to exploit a TOCTOU window, where symlinks can be substituted after path validation but before mounting, exposing host filesystem contents to the build container.
hint_l1: Build execution and container filesystem mount management
hint_l2: A race condition exists in how cache mount subpaths are resolved during parallel build step execution. When two build steps share the same cache mount with subpaths, the path resolution is not atomic, creating a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) window. An attacker could exploit this by manipulating symlinks between path validation and the actual mount operation, causing files from the host system to become accessible to the build container.

reason: The cs_winkernel_malloc function adds user-supplied size to sizeof(CS_WINKERNEL_MEMBLOCK) without overflow checking, allowing a crafted large value to wrap around and allocate a smaller-than-expected buffer, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow.
hint_l1: Platform-specific memory allocation and resource management
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in a memory allocation routine where a user-controlled size parameter is added to a fixed structure size without overflow checking. A specially crafted large size value causes the arithmetic to wrap around, resulting in a smaller-than-expected heap allocation. This leads to a heap-based buffer overflow, potentially allowing denial of service or other impacts.

reason: The UseToken function silently ignores all errors from GetTokenID, so ACME and SCEP provisioners (which do not support token flows) have their authorization checks completely bypassed. Additionally, the revocation endpoint does not verify serial number consistency between the request and the authentication credential.
hint_l1: Certificate authority provisioner and request authorization infrastructure
hint_l2: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where the token validation logic silently ignores errors from provisioner types that do not support token-based authentication flows, effectively disabling token reuse protection. This allows an attacker to bypass authorization checks entirely for certain provisioner configurations. Additionally, the certificate revocation path does not verify that the serial number in the request matches the serial number in the authentication credential, enabling unauthorized revocation of arbitrary certificates.

reason: The parser incorrectly uses deferred function stub data while processing formal argument lists, causing out-of-bounds memory access when arrow functions or nested functions appear within function parameters.
hint_l1: Script parsing and compilation pipeline
hint_l2: A memory corruption vulnerability exists in the JavaScript engine where internal parse-time optimization data structures are incorrectly referenced during function parameter list processing. When crafted JavaScript containing nested constructs such as arrow functions or function expressions within formal parameters is parsed, this leads to out-of-bounds memory access and potential remote code execution.

reason: The MapFunctionObjectTypes function omits one of the function types when iterating, leaving stale JIT entry points after cleanup. This leads to memory corruption that can be exploited for remote code execution via crafted JavaScript.
hint_l1: JIT compilation and function object lifecycle management
hint_l2: A memory corruption vulnerability exists in the scripting engine's handling of function object types during entry point cleanup. A specific function type is not included when iterating over all registered types, causing stale JIT entry points to persist after garbage collection. An attacker can exploit this via crafted script to achieve remote code execution.

reason: User-supplied name and surname fields are stored without sanitization and rendered without output encoding in the backend users listing page, allowing persistent XSS that executes in the context of authenticated administrative users.
hint_l1: Backend administration and content management modules
hint_l2: A stored cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where user-controlled input fields are persisted to the database without proper sanitization and subsequently rendered in an administrative interface without output encoding. An attacker can inject persistent JavaScript payloads that execute automatically in the context of authenticated administrative users, enabling session hijacking, privilege escalation, and full administrative account compromise.

reason: The blog category title field lacks sufficient input validation and output encoding, allowing an attacker to inject and persist a malicious JavaScript payload that executes when the category title is rendered in public and administrative views.
hint_l1: Blog content management and frontend rendering
hint_l2: A stored cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where user-controlled input in a content management field is persisted server-side without adequate sanitization. The stored payload is subsequently rendered across multiple public-facing and administrative pages without proper output encoding, allowing execution of arbitrary JavaScript in victims' browsers. This can lead to privilege escalation and full account takeover across all roles.

reason: The variable-length integer read and write functions use Py_ssize_t (a signed, platform-dependent type) for accumulating results and shifts, allowing a malicious server to trigger an integer overflow that leads to a buffer overflow and potential arbitrary code execution on the client.
hint_l1: Data serialization and wire protocol handling layer
hint_l2: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the binary data encoding and decoding routines used for client-server protocol communication. A malicious server can send crafted responses that cause integer overflow due to the use of insufficiently sized signed integer types in the parsing logic, potentially leading to a crash or arbitrary code execution on the client.

reason: User-supplied configuration arrays from HTTP request body parameters are passed unsanitized to the framework's component instantiation, allowing an attacker to inject event handlers or behaviors that execute arbitrary PHP code on the server.
hint_l1: Server-side request processing and dynamic component configuration
hint_l2: A code injection vulnerability allows unauthenticated remote attackers to execute arbitrary PHP code on the server. User-supplied data from HTTP request parameters is passed directly to the application's internal component instantiation mechanism without sanitization. An attacker can craft malicious configuration payloads that leverage the framework's component configuration features to attach and execute arbitrary code, resulting in full remote code execution.

reason: The table row parsing logic increments a uint16_t column counter without checking for overflow past UINT16_MAX, causing an integer wraparound that leads to heap memory corruption when processing tables with more than 65535 columns.
hint_l1: Markdown parsing extensions and native C bindings
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the parsing of markdown table marker rows. When a table contains more than UINT16_MAX (65535) columns, the column counter wraps around, leading to heap memory corruption. The impact ranges from information disclosure to arbitrary code execution when processing attacker-controlled markdown input.

reason: BitSet signer indices are cast from usize to u16 without bounds checking, allowing out-of-range indices (spaced by 65536) to inflate the apparent signer count while colliding onto the same in-range slot, bypassing the quorum threshold.
hint_l1: Consensus block proof verification and validator quorum checking
hint_l2: An integer truncation vulnerability exists in the blockchain consensus layer. Signer slot indices from a bit set are cast from a larger integer type to a smaller one without range validation. An attacker can craft a proof containing out-of-range signer indices that, after truncation, collide with valid in-range slots. This inflates the apparent number of unique signers, allowing a malicious validator to bypass the Byzantine fault tolerance quorum threshold with far fewer actual signing keys.

reason: The SchemaFactory used for XML schema validation does not have secure processing enabled, allowing XML External Entity (XXE) attacks via crafted XML input.
hint_l1: XML document parsing and processing utilities
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability exists where an XML processing component does not enable secure processing restrictions. An attacker can supply a crafted XML document that references external entities, potentially leading to disclosure of confidential data, server-side request forgery, or denial of service.

reason: The maxDecBitLen constant used for range validation in decimal arithmetic operations was misaligned with MaxBitLen used for integers, allowing decimal values that exceed the integer type's valid range and causing panics during type interactions.
hint_l1: Mathematical data types and arithmetic operations
hint_l2: A misalignment in the maximum allowed bit-length between integer and decimal data types causes an inconsistency in range validation. Decimal values can be created that fit within the decimal type's validation bounds but exceed the valid range of the corresponding integer type. When such an oversized decimal value is used in an integer context, it causes a panic. An attacker can exploit this by providing crafted numeric inputs that produce values within the incorrectly permissive decimal range but outside the actual safe limits, leading to a denial of service through an unrecoverable panic.

reason: User-controlled cron expression input is passed via exception getMessage() into a Bean Validation constraint violation message template, which evaluates embedded Java EL expressions, enabling arbitrary remote code execution.
hint_l1: Annotation processing and constraint evaluation
hint_l2: A template injection vulnerability allows attackers to inject arbitrary Java Expression Language (EL) expressions, leading to unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE). User-controlled input is passed unsanitized into a message template that evaluates embedded EL expressions at runtime. This enables an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the server by crafting malicious input strings containing EL payloads.

reason: The randomDigits() function divides each random byte by 25.6 and floors the result to produce a digit 0-9, but since 256 is not evenly divisible by 10 this creates a biased distribution where some digits are more likely than others, undermining the cryptographic strength of generated values.
hint_l1: Random value generation and cryptographic utility functions
hint_l2: A random digit generation routine uses a flawed mapping from random bytes to decimal digits, producing a biased distribution. The conversion arithmetic does not evenly partition the byte value space across the ten possible digit outputs, causing some digits to appear more frequently than others over a large number of invocations.

reason: When pkcs12.serialize_key_and_certificates is called with a mismatched key and certificate alongside an hmac_hash override, the PKCS12_create call returns NULL but the result is not checked, leading to a NULL pointer dereference that crashes the Python process.
hint_l1: Cryptographic key and certificate serialization routines
hint_l2: A NULL pointer dereference occurs when serializing a PKCS#12 bundle with a private key that does not match the provided certificate and a custom MAC hash algorithm. The underlying library call returns NULL on failure, but the result is used without validation, causing the process to crash.

reason: The cloudstore file upload action writes user-supplied filenames directly to disk without sanitizing directory traversal sequences, allowing unauthenticated attackers to perform path traversal and zip slip attacks for arbitrary file write.
hint_l1: Cloud storage and file upload processing
hint_l2: A path traversal and zip slip vulnerability exists where user-supplied filenames are written directly to disk without proper sanitization during file upload operations. An unauthenticated attacker can craft filenames containing directory traversal sequences to write arbitrary files outside the intended storage directory, potentially leading to remote code execution.

reason: Certain FFI operations for calling native function pointers did not enforce permission or stability checks, allowing code within the sandbox to bypass the runtime's permission model and execute arbitrary native code.
hint_l1: Runtime extension module operation registration and dispatch
hint_l2: A sandbox escape vulnerability exists in the runtime where certain low-level operations for interacting with native code and memory fail to enforce required permission and stability checks. A malicious actor controlling code within the sandboxed execution environment can bypass the permission model by directly invoking these ungated operations, enabling arbitrary code execution even when the necessary dangerous capabilities have not been explicitly granted by the user.

reason: The interactive permission prompt does not lock standard I/O streams, allowing concurrent threads (e.g., Web Workers) to overwrite the prompt with misleading text and trick users into granting unintended permissions.
hint_l1: Runtime permission prompting and user interaction subsystem
hint_l2: A race condition vulnerability exists in the interactive permission prompt mechanism. In a multi-threaded context, concurrent code can write to standard output and error streams while a security-sensitive prompt is being displayed to the user. This allows a malicious program to clear the terminal screen and replace the permission prompt with misleading text, tricking the user into granting permissions they did not intend to grant.

reason: The SQLite backend stores column name string references obtained via sqlite3_column_name before calling sqlite3_step, but the SQLite documentation states these pointers are invalidated by the first sqlite3_step call, resulting in a use-after-free.
hint_l1: Database connection and query processing layer for the SQLite backend
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in the SQLite backend of an ORM library. When processing query results by column name, the code stores references to column name strings returned by the SQLite C API. However, these references become invalid after the first call to step through the result set, as the SQLite documentation states that the returned string pointer is only valid until the statement is stepped. Subsequent use of these invalidated references constitutes a use-after-free, potentially leading to memory corruption, crashes, or information disclosure.

reason: The archive extraction utilities do not strip leading file separator characters from entry names, allowing archives with absolute paths to write files to arbitrary system locations.
hint_l1: Model artifact retrieval and resource management utilities
hint_l2: An absolute path traversal vulnerability exists where archived artifacts with entries containing absolute path names are not properly sanitized during extraction. A crafted archive can include entries with leading file separator characters, causing files to be written to arbitrary locations on the filesystem instead of the intended target directory, potentially overwriting critical system files.

reason: The DNS request class uses System.Random, a non-cryptographic PRNG, to generate request identifiers, allowing an attacker to predict IDs and spoof DNS responses.
hint_l1: DNS protocol request construction and message handling
hint_l2: A DNS client library uses a non-cryptographic pseudorandom number generator to produce request identifiers, making them predictable. An attacker who can observe or guess the sequence of identifiers could craft forged DNS responses that are accepted as legitimate, enabling DNS cache poisoning or response spoofing.

reason: The rowid parameter in the subscription info page is taken directly from unsanitized GET/POST input and used in database queries, allowing remote SQL injection.
hint_l1: Membership and subscription record management
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where a user-supplied HTTP request parameter is directly used in database operations without proper type enforcement or sanitization, allowing remote unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL commands.

reason: The Kerberos authenticator uses ThreadLocalRandom (a non-cryptographically-secure PRNG) to generate a fallback cookie signature secret when the configuration is not explicitly set, allowing an attacker to predict or brute-force the secret and forge authentication tokens.
hint_l1: Security and authentication extensions
hint_l2: A cryptographic weakness exists where a non-cryptographically-secure pseudo-random number generator is used to generate a fallback secret for signing authentication tokens when the relevant security configuration is not explicitly set. An attacker could predict or brute-force the generated secret, potentially enabling token forgery or authentication bypass.

reason: The DocumentBuilderFactory used to parse XML sign responses and metadata does not disable doctype declarations, allowing XML External Entity injection via crafted XML input.
hint_l1: XML response parsing and signature verification in the digital signature service client
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) injection vulnerability exists in the XML parsing logic used to process digital signature service responses and metadata. The XML parser does not disable external entity resolution or doctype declarations, allowing an attacker to inject crafted XML payloads that reference external entities. This can lead to disclosure of internal files, server-side request forgery, or denial of service.

reason: Error objects exposed to sandboxed code had their prototype chain incompletely severed, allowing sandbox code to traverse to the host Function constructor and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the host runtime.
hint_l1: Sandbox runtime execution and security enforcement layer
hint_l2: A code injection vulnerability exists where untrusted sandboxed code can escape the sandbox by exploiting incomplete isolation of host-realm objects. When certain operations fail, objects with intact prototype chains from the host environment are exposed to sandboxed code. An attacker can traverse these prototype chains to reach the host's code generation facilities and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the host Node.js runtime, bypassing all sandbox isolation guarantees.

reason: The custom JSON deserializer uses a __type__ field to dynamically import and instantiate arbitrary classes with attacker-controlled arguments, enabling remote code execution via classes such as subprocess.Popen.
hint_l1: Data serialization and deserialization infrastructure
hint_l2: An insecure deserialization vulnerability exists in the JSON parsing logic. When processing incoming JSON data, the deserializer honors a special type annotation field that allows an attacker to specify arbitrary modules and classes to be dynamically imported and instantiated with attacker-controlled arguments. This can be exploited to invoke dangerous system-level classes, leading to arbitrary command execution.

reason: The XML parser in the document utility method creates a DocumentBuilderFactory without disabling external entity resolution or doctype declarations, allowing XML Entity Expansion and XXE attacks via crafted spreadsheet files.
hint_l1: Spreadsheet file parsing and data extraction logic
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) and XML Entity Expansion vulnerability exists where the XML parser used during spreadsheet processing does not disable doctype declarations, external entity resolution, or entity reference expansion. A crafted input file could trigger XML entity expansion, leading to denial of service, information disclosure, or server-side request forgery.

reason: The REST server's XML parser creates an XMLReader via SAXParserFactory without disabling external entity processing, allowing attackers to submit crafted XML that triggers XXE attacks leading to data disclosure, SSRF, and denial of service.
hint_l1: HTTP request handling and server-side document processing
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability exists in the XML parser used for processing incoming REST API requests. The parser does not properly restrict external entity references, allowing an attacker to send crafted XML payloads that can result in disclosure of confidential data, server-side request forgery (SSRF), port scanning, and denial of service.

reason: The signup handler blindly applies all default user settings, including the admin permission flag, to newly registered accounts without stripping elevated privileges, allowing any unauthenticated visitor to register as a full administrator.
hint_l1: User authentication and account management
hint_l2: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where newly created user accounts through the public registration feature inherit all configured default permissions — including administrative privileges — without any server-side restriction. When public registration is enabled and the default user configuration includes elevated access, any unauthenticated visitor can create an account that is automatically granted full administrative control over the application, including file management, user management, and server configuration.

reason: The convertToValidJSONString function uses the Function() constructor to evaluate user-provided MCP server configuration strings, allowing arbitrary JavaScript code execution with full Node.js runtime privileges.
hint_l1: External tool and server configuration processing logic
hint_l2: A code injection vulnerability exists where user-provided server configuration input is processed using a dynamic code evaluation mechanism rather than a safe data parser. An attacker can craft a malicious configuration string that, when submitted via the API, causes arbitrary JavaScript to execute with full Node.js runtime privileges, enabling system command execution, file system access, and data exfiltration.

reason: The translation system's parameter preprocessing returns user-supplied string inputs without sanitization, allowing them to be rendered as HTML DOM nodes. This enables any user to inject arbitrary HTML and JavaScript through input fields like the search box.
hint_l1: Frontend internationalization and content rendering layer
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting vulnerability exists in the forum software where user-provided string inputs are converted into HTML DOM nodes when rendered in the UI. This allows any user to inject malicious HTML markup through user input fields, which executes in other users' browsers. The attack could be used to perform AJAX requests on behalf of victims, potentially deleting content, modifying settings, or compromising administrator accounts.

reason: The token refresh endpoint mints new JWTs from an attacker-controlled HTTP header without verifying the caller is authenticated, allowing unauthenticated attackers to forge administrator tokens and achieve remote code execution.
hint_l1: Authentication and session token management
hint_l2: An authentication bypass vulnerability allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to mint administrative JSON Web Tokens by supplying attacker-controlled identity data through HTTP request headers during a token renewal flow. The server trusts user-supplied identity claims without verifying them against a valid authenticated session, enabling arbitrary privilege escalation and subsequent remote code execution through administrative APIs.

reason: The project uses the satori/go.uuid library which employs math/rand instead of crypto/rand for UUID generation, making generated UUIDs predictable to an attacker who can observe prior outputs.
hint_l1: Database replication and data synchronization infrastructure
hint_l2: A cryptographic weakness exists where UUID identifiers are generated using an insecure pseudo-random number generator. The underlying randomness source is not cryptographically secure, making generated identifiers predictable to an attacker who can observe prior outputs. This could allow an attacker to forge or predict identity values used in replication protocols.

reason: The path validation function fails to account for Windows treating a trailing dot as equivalent to the original directory name, allowing attackers to bypass the upload restriction and place a crafted config file that enables arbitrary command execution via SSH access.
hint_l1: Repository file upload and content management
hint_l2: A command injection vulnerability exists in the repository file upload functionality. On Windows, a path validation check can be bypassed by appending a trailing dot to a restricted directory name, allowing an attacker to upload crafted configuration files into that directory. This enables arbitrary command execution on the server when repository operations are performed.

reason: The oauth_redirect URL parameter is decoded and passed directly to location.assign() without any validation, allowing an attacker to inject a javascript: URI and execute arbitrary scripts in the user's browser.
hint_l1: OAuth authentication flow and redirect handling
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where a URL parameter controlling an OAuth redirect destination is passed directly to a browser navigation call without validation or sanitization. An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious redirect value using a dangerous protocol scheme such as javascript:, which will be executed in the user's browser context when processed.

reason: In the transient object property assignment error path, a single temporary allocator was shared between two UTF-16 string conversions, causing the first returned reference to be invalidated when the second conversion appended to the same allocator, resulting in use of freed memory.
hint_l1: Virtual machine runtime and bytecode interpreter infrastructure
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in the JavaScript engine where, during construction of a runtime error message, a reference to a converted string buffer is invalidated by a subsequent string conversion that reuses the same temporary storage. An attacker can trigger this condition through crafted JavaScript that provokes a specific type error, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. This is only exploitable if the application permits evaluation of untrusted JavaScript.

reason: The Save method performed a safety check and state update under separate locks, allowing two concurrent signing requests for the same height-round-step to both pass the check before either wrote the updated state, resulting in a double-sign.
hint_l1: Cryptographic signing state management and persistence
hint_l2: A race condition exists in the consensus signature state handling. When two signing requests arrive nearly simultaneously for the same block height, round, and step, a split read-write lock pattern allows both requests to pass safety checks before either updates the state. This enables conflicting signatures to be produced for the same block, constituting a double-sign protocol violation that results in stake slashing penalties.

reason: User-controlled OAuth GET request parameters are reflected into HTML output without escaping, allowing XSS and XWiki syntax injection that enables remote code execution via Groovy macros.
hint_l1: OAuth-based authentication and identity provider integration UI
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists in the OAuth login flow where user-controlled parameters sent via a GET request are reflected into the rendered HTML page without proper output escaping. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript and wiki syntax from the login screen, enabling remote code execution via script macros. The vulnerability affects the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the entire installation.

reason: The credential name from instance configuration is used to construct a file path via filepath.Join without sanitization, allowing directory traversal sequences in credential names to write arbitrary files on the host filesystem.
hint_l1: Container instance lifecycle and configuration management
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists in the container credential provisioning mechanism. When writing credential files for guest system services, user-controlled credential names are used directly in file path construction without validation or confinement. An attacker with access to instance configuration can craft credential names containing directory traversal sequences to write arbitrary files on the host filesystem as root, enabling privilege escalation or denial of service.

reason: The From<Buffer> for Vec<u8> implementation creates a Vec from raw parts of a slice but does not call mem::forget on the slice, allowing it to be dropped and its memory freed while the Vec still references it.
hint_l1: Data buffering and memory management utilities
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in a data conversion routine where raw pointer ownership is transferred to a new allocation, but the original memory owner is not prevented from deallocating the backing storage. This results in the returned data structure being backed by freed memory, leading to potential memory corruption or undefined behavior.

reason: User-controlled message strings are passed to a JavaScript alert call in generated HTML without HTML encoding, allowing injection of arbitrary script content via crafted input.
hint_l1: HTML report generation and rendering layer
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where user-controlled message content is embedded into dynamically generated HTML output without proper encoding, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary scripts that execute in the context of other users' browsers.

reason: The sanitizeString method in JsonSanitizer uses direct charAt() lookups instead of decoding escape sequences when checking for dangerous HTML/XML patterns like <script, <!--, -->, and ]]>, allowing crafted inputs with escape sequences to bypass output encoding checks and inject arbitrary HTML or XML into embedding documents.
hint_l1: JSON content processing and output sanitization
hint_l2: A vulnerability exists where crafted JSON-like input can cause the sanitizer to emit HTML and XML control sequences in its output, including closing script element markers and CDATA section delimiters. The sanitizer fails to properly account for character escape sequences when detecting dangerous patterns within string content, allowing attackers to bypass output encoding and inject arbitrary HTML or XML into documents that embed the sanitized output.

reason: The cloud specification API endpoint returns cloud credentials to any authenticated client without verifying that the caller has superuser or model admin privileges, allowing unauthorized credential disclosure.
hint_l1: Cloud infrastructure management and controller API services
hint_l2: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where any authenticated user with basic login permission can retrieve sensitive cloud infrastructure credentials by calling an API endpoint with a known model identifier. The endpoint does not verify that the caller has elevated privileges such as superuser or administrative permissions before returning confidential credential details in the response, allowing unauthorized access to bootstrap credentials.

reason: The redirect URI validation does not check that the URI scheme is restricted to HTTP(S), allowing an attacker to inject a JavaScript URI via a crafted SAML or OIDC request, resulting in cross-site scripting when the URI is used in a form POST response.
hint_l1: Authentication protocol response and redirect processing
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where redirect URIs in authentication protocol requests are not validated against allowed URI schemes. An attacker can craft a request containing a redirect URI with a dangerous scheme (such as a JavaScript URI), which is then used in a form-based POST response without proper sanitization, allowing execution of arbitrary scripts in the victim's browser session.

reason: The HPA controller dereferences ScaleUp and ScaleDown behavior pointers without nil checks, causing a panic when a v1 HPA is created with partially specified behaviors that leave these fields nil after API version conversion.
hint_l1: Workload autoscaling and resource management controllers
hint_l2: A nil pointer dereference vulnerability exists in the controller manager. When a HorizontalPodAutoscaler object is created or modified through the v1 API with partially specified scaling behavior configuration (e.g., specifying scale-down behavior but omitting scale-up behavior), the controller does not validate that all required behavior fields are populated before accessing them. This results in a nil pointer dereference that causes the controller manager to panic and enter a crash loop, leading to denial of service.

reason: The MultiDecoder's read implementation uses mem::replace with mem::uninitialized() to temporarily move the decoder state, creating a use-after-free if a panic occurs before valid state is restored.
hint_l1: Data stream decompression and decoding pipeline
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in the multi-stream decompression logic. An unsafe block uses uninitialized memory as a temporary placeholder when swapping a decoder's internal state. If a panic occurs between placing the uninitialized value and restoring valid state, the uninitialized memory may be read, leading to arbitrary code execution.

reason: The cookie retrieval method uses PHP's unserialize() on user-controlled cookie values without validation, enabling PHP object injection and arbitrary code execution via magic methods like __wakeup().
hint_l1: HTTP cookie management and data persistence layer
hint_l2: An insecure deserialization vulnerability exists where user-controlled cookie values are deserialized using an unsafe mechanism without proper validation, allowing PHP object injection. An attacker can craft a malicious cookie payload that, when processed by the application, triggers arbitrary code execution through magic methods during the deserialization process.

reason: The signature parsing functions accept R and S scalar values that exceed the curve order without returning an error, allowing invalid overflowing signatures to pass verification.
hint_l1: Elliptic curve signature handling and verification
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the signature parsing logic of a SECP256K1 library. The signature components are not validated against the curve order during parsing, allowing values larger than the curve order to be accepted. This enables an attacker to craft signatures that should be invalid but pass verification, undermining the integrity of cryptographic signature validation.

reason: The run_sql_query function executes user-supplied SQL strings directly against the database engine via SQLAlchemy's text() without any sanitization or parameterization, enabling arbitrary SQL injection and remote code execution on PostgreSQL through large object functionality.
hint_l1: Agent tool specifications and database query handling
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where user-controlled query strings are passed directly to the database engine for execution without any sanitization or parameterization. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL queries, which on PostgreSQL databases can lead to remote code execution through large object functionality.

reason: User-controlled HTTP request parameters are directly interpolated into HQL query strings in the calendar JSON service, allowing unauthenticated SQL injection that can leak database contents or cause denial of service.
hint_l1: Calendar event data retrieval and JSON API service endpoints
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where user-controlled HTTP request parameters are directly interpolated into database query strings without sanitization or parameterized binding. An attacker with view access, including unauthenticated guest users, can inject arbitrary SQL to access database information or perform denial of service attacks.

reason: The _validate_source function does not check for relative path components in non-local source URIs, allowing path traversal attacks via crafted URIs containing '../' sequences to access arbitrary server files.
hint_l1: Model registry and tracking server request handling
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists where URIs provided for model artifact sources are not validated for relative path components (e.g., '../'). An attacker can craft a URI with relative path sequences to traverse the server's file system and access or reference files outside the intended directory. This affects URIs using HTTP, HTTPS, and custom artifact protocol schemes.

reason: The filename from the Content-Disposition HTTP header is used to build a local file path without sanitization, allowing an attacker-controlled server to write arbitrary files via path traversal sequences.
hint_l1: Dataset loading and remote data source retrieval
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists where a filename extracted from a remote server's HTTP response header is used to construct a local file path without proper sanitization. An attacker controlling the server can supply a crafted filename containing path traversal sequences or absolute paths, leading to arbitrary file write on the local system, which can result in remote code execution.

reason: The path validation function does not decode URL-encoded input before checking for traversal sequences, allowing attackers to bypass safety checks using percent-encoded characters like %2E%2E%2F to access arbitrary files.
hint_l1: Artifact management and tracking server infrastructure
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists where URL-encoded directory traversal sequences are not properly decoded before safety checks are applied. An attacker can use percent-encoded characters to traverse outside the intended directory, potentially accessing or modifying arbitrary files on the server.

reason: The server's path validation function fails to reject Windows drive-relative paths, allowing an attacker to PUT arbitrary files outside the intended artifact directory via crafted path parameters.
hint_l1: Server-side API endpoint handlers and request processing
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists where insufficient validation of user-supplied file paths allows an attacker to bypass directory traversal protections. By using OS-specific path representations in HTTP requests, an attacker can write arbitrary files to locations outside the intended directory on the server.

reason: The list_artifacts method in the HTTP artifact repository processes file paths from a remote server response without validation, allowing path traversal to write arbitrary files and achieve command execution.
hint_l1: Artifact storage and retrieval subsystem
hint_l2: A vulnerability exists where artifact file paths received from a remote server response are used without proper validation in file system operations. An attacker who controls the server response can supply specially crafted paths to write or overwrite arbitrary files on the host filesystem, enabling remote code execution through overwriting configuration files such as shell initialization scripts.

reason: Form validation error messages are rendered using an unsafe template filter that bypasses HTML escaping, allowing attacker-controlled input to be reflected as executable script in the browser.
hint_l1: Frontend templating and client-side form handling
hint_l2: A DOM-based Cross-site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability exists where form validation error messages are rendered without proper HTML escaping. An attacker can inject malicious scripts through crafted input that, when rejected by form validation, gets reflected back to the user's browser unescaped, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution.

reason: The h5p activity external web service accepts a sort order parameter and passes it directly into SQL queries without validation, enabling SQL injection by remote attackers.
hint_l1: Activity module external web service API layer
hint_l2: An SQL injection vulnerability exists where a user-controlled sort order parameter passed through a web service API is directly incorporated into database queries without proper validation or sanitization, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary SQL commands.

reason: Multiple XML project file readers create SAXParserFactory and XMLReader instances without disabling doctype declarations or external entity processing, allowing XXE attacks via crafted project files.
hint_l1: XML-based project file reading and deserialization
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability exists in project file parsing components. When processing XML-based project files, the XML parser does not disable external entity resolution or doctype declarations, allowing an attacker to supply a crafted project file that triggers XXE attacks, potentially leading to information disclosure, server-side request forgery, or denial of service.

reason: The automatically derived Deserialize implementation for VecStorage does not validate that the number of deserialized elements equals nrows * ncols, allowing crafted inputs to cause out-of-bounds memory access.
hint_l1: Matrix data storage and serialization layer
hint_l2: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in the deserialization logic for dynamically-sized matrix storage. The automatically derived deserialization implementation does not validate that the number of elements in the deserialized data matches the expected dimensions (rows times columns). An attacker can craft malicious serialized input where the claimed dimensions exceed the actual data length, leading to out-of-bounds memory access when the matrix is subsequently used.

reason: The JWT validation routines lack nil checks for import and export entries in user claims. A maliciously crafted JWT with null import or export entries causes a nil pointer dereference during validation, resulting in a server panic and denial of service.
hint_l1: Account claims processing and token validation logic
hint_l2: A nil pointer dereference vulnerability exists in JWT claim validation. When processing a maliciously crafted user JWT containing null fields in its claim structures, the validation routine attempts to dereference nil pointers, causing a panic and process termination. A remote attacker with the ability to issue account tokens can trigger a denial of service by submitting a specially crafted JWT.

reason: The SplitXml processor creates a SAX parser without disabling external general entity processing or disallowing DOCTYPE declarations, allowing XXE attacks through crafted XML input.
hint_l1: Data flow content processing and transformation
hint_l2: An XML External Entity (XXE) vulnerability exists where a SAX parser used to process XML content does not disable external general entity resolution or disallow DOCTYPE declarations. An attacker can supply crafted malicious XML input to cause information disclosure or remote code execution.

reason: The Unicode normalization function uses NFD form which does not collapse ligatures like the sharp-s character into their multi-character equivalents, causing the path reservation system to fail to detect colliding paths on normalization-insensitive filesystems and enabling a TOCTOU race condition.
hint_l1: Archive entry extraction and path processing pipeline
hint_l2: A race condition vulnerability exists due to incomplete Unicode normalization of file paths during archive extraction. On filesystems that are case-insensitive or normalization-insensitive, certain Unicode characters such as ligatures can collide with their multi-character equivalents at the filesystem level, but the library's internal concurrency control system does not recognize these collisions. This allows archive entries with colliding paths to be processed in parallel rather than being serialized, bypassing internal safeguards and enabling symlink poisoning attacks that can lead to arbitrary file overwrites.

reason: The TLS configuration for RPC connections only verified that client certificates were signed by the correct CA and had not expired, but did not validate the certificate's role and region fields, allowing privilege escalation via certificate impersonation.
hint_l1: RPC transport layer and inter-node connection security configuration
hint_l2: An improper certificate validation vulnerability exists where TLS certificates used for mutual TLS (mTLS) RPC communication are not correctly validated for their associated role and region. A certificate signed by a trusted CA but bearing incorrect role or region claims is accepted without proper verification, allowing an attacker to escalate privileges by impersonating a different node type or accessing services in a region they are not authorized for.

reason: The deep link handler renders a user-controlled hostname parameter directly into the editor's UI without validating or sanitizing it, allowing injection of HTML and script content that executes in the editor's context.
hint_l1: Editor plugin integration and external navigation handling
hint_l2: A cross-site scripting vulnerability exists where a user-controlled parameter from a URI-based navigation request is rendered directly into the application's UI context without sanitization. An attacker can craft a malicious URL containing HTML or script content in the parameter, which gets rendered in the application's context and could potentially be chained to lead to code execution.

reason: NumPy's data loading functions default to allow_pickle=True, enabling deserialization of arbitrary Python objects via pickle. An attacker can craft a malicious serialized file that executes arbitrary code when loaded.
hint_l1: Data persistence and file loading infrastructure
hint_l2: An insecure deserialization vulnerability exists where loading data from untrusted sources permits arbitrary code execution. The default configuration allows deserialization of arbitrary Python objects from crafted serialized data, enabling remote code execution when a user loads a malicious file.

reason: The request URL path is not normalized before rule matching, allowing path traversal sequences to bypass access control rules and reach protected upstream resources.
hint_l1: HTTP request processing and access rule matching
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists where an attacker can craft HTTP requests containing relative path components that bypass access control rules. The raw, un-normalized request path is used during rule evaluation, allowing requests to match permissive rules while the upstream server normalizes the path and serves protected resources.

reason: The shell safety check does not block the password argument type, allowing shell metacharacter injection via sh -c. Webhook-extracted JSON values also skip type safety checks entirely, enabling unauthenticated command injection.
hint_l1: Action execution pipeline and external trigger handling
hint_l2: An OS command injection vulnerability exists where certain argument types bypass shell safety checks, allowing shell metacharacters to be injected into commands executed via sh -c. Additionally, external trigger payloads can inject arbitrary key-value pairs that skip type safety validation entirely before being interpolated into shell commands, enabling unauthenticated remote code execution.

reason: An incomplete authorization check in multi-tenant deployments fails to verify the bearer token's issuer against an allowlist of permitted AAD tenants, allowing any authenticated Azure AD user to make authorized API calls.
hint_l1: API service authentication and authorization layer
hint_l2: An improper authorization vulnerability exists where, in multi-tenant deployments, the API service does not verify that the issuer claim in a bearer token belongs to an allowed identity provider tenant. This allows an authenticated user from any tenant to make authorized API calls, resulting in unauthorized read/write access to private data such as vulnerability information, security testing tools, and proprietary code, as well as enabling tampering with existing data and unauthorized code execution on cloud compute resources.

reason: The telemetry aggregation API accepts user-controlled aggregationType, aggregateColumnName, and aggregationTimestampColumnName parameters and interpolates them directly into ClickHouse SQL queries via raw string concatenation without any allowlist validation or parameterized query binding.
hint_l1: Telemetry data aggregation and analytics query processing
hint_l2: User-controlled query parameters for data aggregation operations are interpolated directly into analytics database queries using raw string concatenation marked as trusted SQL. There is no allowlist validation or parameterized binding for aggregation type, column name, or timestamp column parameters. An authenticated user can inject arbitrary SQL, enabling cross-tenant data theft, data manipulation, and potential remote code execution via database-specific table functions.

reason: The performTraceroute method directly interpolates an attacker-controlled destination string into a shell command template passed to child_process.exec(), which spawns a shell and interprets metacharacters, enabling arbitrary OS command execution.
hint_l1: Network monitoring and diagnostics probe utilities
hint_l2: An OS command injection vulnerability exists where a user-controlled destination string used for network path tracing is directly interpolated into a shell command template and executed via a shell-spawning process execution call without any sanitization or validation. Shell metacharacters in the destination value are interpreted by the spawned shell, allowing authenticated users to execute arbitrary operating system commands on the server.

reason: The gateway WebSocket connection handler did not clear client-declared scopes for shared-token and shared-password authenticated connections, allowing device-less operators to self-declare elevated scopes such as operator.admin and perform administrative operations.
hint_l1: Gateway WebSocket connection and session initialization
hint_l2: An authorization bypass vulnerability exists where certain authenticated WebSocket connections could self-declare elevated privilege scopes. A logic flaw in the scope validation path allowed shared-authentication connections to preserve client-supplied scopes that were not bound to a device identity or explicitly trusted connection type, enabling unauthorized access to administrative operations.

reason: ZIP extraction validates the output path then opens/truncates the file in a separate step, creating a TOCTOU window where a parent directory symlink can be rebound to redirect the write outside the destination root.
hint_l1: File system operations and archive processing infrastructure
hint_l2: A race condition (TOCTOU) vulnerability exists in ZIP archive extraction where the output path is validated and then separately opened and truncated for writing. Between the validation step and the file open/truncate operations, a local attacker can rebind a parent directory via symlink replacement to redirect the final write outside the intended extraction root, allowing arbitrary file overwrites on the host file system.

reason: The command execution approval flow does not freeze immutable plan fields or reject mutable parent-symlink path components in the working directory, allowing an attacker to rebind a parent symlink between approval and execution to redirect command execution to an arbitrary filesystem location.
hint_l1: Command execution approval and sandboxing infrastructure
hint_l2: A race condition vulnerability exists in the command execution approval flow for node-hosted processes. Between the time a command execution request is approved and when it is actually executed, an attacker can rebind a mutable parent symlink in the working directory path, causing the approved command to execute from a different filesystem location than what was inspected at approval time. The visible working directory string remains unchanged, bypassing the approval context check.

reason: The plugin's client-deny handler returns a success status code instead of an error, causing the VPN server to treat denied clients as authenticated and granting them full network access without completing OIDC authentication.
hint_l1: VPN client authentication and session handling
hint_l2: An authentication bypass vulnerability exists in the experimental plugin mode where VPN clients that do not support web-based single sign-on are incorrectly granted full network access. When a client is denied authentication, the plugin returns a synchronous success status code to the VPN server instead of an error or deferred status. Because the VPN server only consults the authentication control file when the plugin defers, the synchronous success return is interpreted as immediate approval, admitting the unauthenticated client to the internal network.

reason: The rand function uses Math.random() instead of a cryptographically secure random number generator to select characters for one-time passwords, making the generated OTPs predictable and vulnerable to brute-force attacks.
hint_l1: One-time password creation and character selection logic
hint_l2: The package uses a non-cryptographic pseudorandom number generator to select characters when creating one-time passwords, making the generated passwords predictable and vulnerable to brute-force attacks.

reason: The key-value pair iterator creates uninitialized memory buffers using Vec::with_capacity followed by an unsafe set_len, then passes them to a user-provided Read implementation, exposing uninitialized heap memory.
hint_l1: CGI and FastCGI protocol data parsing and record processing
hint_l2: A buffer is allocated with a specified capacity and then unsafely marked as having its full length without initializing its contents. This uninitialized buffer is subsequently passed to a user-provided reader implementation. When the reader processes this buffer, it may read from uninitialized memory, leading to undefined behavior and potential information disclosure.

reason: Schema parsing allows unrestricted instantiation of arbitrary Java classes specified in schema metadata, enabling remote code execution through crafted data files.
hint_l1: Data serialization schema processing and record conversion components
hint_l2: An insecure deserialization vulnerability exists in the schema parsing logic where Java classes referenced via schema metadata annotations can be instantiated without restriction. An attacker can craft a malicious schema that specifies arbitrary class names, leading to remote code execution when data containing such schemas is read and deserialized. There is no validation or allowlisting of which packages or classes are permitted to be instantiated through schema-defined type references.

reason: The TIFF decoding function multiplies image dimension parameters (tile dimensions or rows-per-strip) by the row byte size to compute a reallocation size without checking for integer overflow, allowing a crafted TIFF to trigger a heap buffer overflow via a wrapped allocation size.
hint_l1: Image codec and decoding routines
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the TIFF image decoding logic where a multiplication of image dimension parameters derived from the file header is used to compute a memory reallocation size without first checking for overflow. A crafted TIFF file with very large dimension values can cause the multiplication to wrap around, leading to a smaller-than-expected buffer allocation followed by an out-of-bounds write.

reason: Unbounded sprintf calls with attacker-controlled mode string parameters can overflow a fixed-size static buffer in the image conversion error path, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution.
hint_l1: Image mode conversion and color space transformation processing
hint_l2: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists where attacker-controlled parameters passed to an image conversion function can overflow a fixed-size stack buffer through unbounded string formatting operations, potentially allowing arbitrary code execution.

reason: An integer overflow in the horizontal image resampling function allows negative or very large size values to bypass allocation size calculations, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow when writing resampled pixel data.
hint_l1: Image resampling and transformation pipeline
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the image resampling logic. When processing images with attacker-controlled dimensions, certain size parameters are not validated before being used in arithmetic operations for memory allocation. This can result in a heap-based buffer overflow, potentially allowing remote attackers to cause a denial of service or execute arbitrary code via crafted image dimensions.

reason: The SGI RLE decoder's expandrow and expandrow2 functions do not validate the run-length count against image dimensions, allowing a crafted SGI image to write beyond the allocated buffer.
hint_l1: Image format decoding and decompression
hint_l2: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in an image format's run-length encoding decoder. The decompression routine does not properly validate run-length counts against the output buffer size, allowing an attacker to trigger a buffer overrun and potentially execute arbitrary code via a crafted image file.

reason: The extractState method deserializes user-supplied data from the _state request parameter using PHP's unserialize() without proper validation, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code via crafted serialized payloads.
hint_l1: Request handling and server-side state management
hint_l2: An insecure deserialization vulnerability exists where a request parameter containing serialized state data is deserialized without proper validation. An attacker can craft a malicious serialized payload that, when processed, results in remote code execution.

reason: The user model's name attribute is a text column with no length validation, allowing unlimited-length input that can cause integer overflow or wraparound when processed.
hint_l1: User account management and data persistence layer
hint_l2: An integer overflow or wraparound vulnerability exists because a text attribute has no length constraint. By supplying an excessively long string value, an attacker can trigger an integer overflow condition during processing, potentially leading to denial of service or other security impacts.

reason: The array conversion routine allocates a fixed-size buffer based on the initial array length but iterates using the live array length, which can grow during conversion, causing heap overflow via out-of-bounds writes.
hint_l1: Data marshalling between the scripting engine and the host language runtime
hint_l2: A heap overflow vulnerability exists where a buffer is allocated based on an initial collection size, but iteration over the collection uses a dynamically re-evaluated length that may have grown since allocation, leading to out-of-bounds writes and potential heap corruption.

reason: The escape_dict function in PyMySQL escapes dictionary values but not dictionary keys, allowing SQL injection when untrusted JSON input with crafted keys is used as query parameters.
hint_l1: Query parameter serialization and encoding
hint_l2: A SQL injection vulnerability exists where dictionary keys from untrusted JSON input are not properly sanitized before being used in SQL queries. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through specially crafted key names in dictionary parameters, potentially leading to unauthorized data access or modification.

reason: The memcache encryption middleware uses weak cryptographic primitives including SHA1 for MAC computation, a simple SHA256 hash for key derivation instead of a proper KDF, and non-constant-time comparison for signature verification, allowing attackers to bypass cache data protection.
hint_l1: Middleware for caching and securing authentication tokens
hint_l2: The middleware uses weak cryptographic primitives for protecting cached authentication data. It employs an inadequate hashing algorithm for computing message authentication codes, uses a simplistic key derivation scheme instead of a proper key derivation function, and does not perform constant-time comparison when verifying signatures, making it vulnerable to timing attacks and data forgery.

reason: When the QUIC handshake completes, the Handshake packet number space is dropped immediately. If an ACK frame is serialized after the CRYPTO frame in the same packet, processing that ACK attempts to access the already-dropped packet number space, causing a nil pointer dereference and panic.
hint_l1: QUIC connection lifecycle and handshake state management
hint_l2: A nil pointer dereference vulnerability exists where a specially ordered sequence of frames within a handshake packet causes the protocol implementation to prematurely release internal state. When subsequent frames in the same packet attempt to access this already-released state, a nil pointer dereference occurs, leading to a panic that crashes the node. An attacker can trigger this with minimal effort, as it only requires completing the QUIC handshake.

reason: The read_u32_into and read_u64_into functions have buffer length assertions with the size multiplier on the wrong side, making them too permissive and allowing random number generators to be seeded with insufficient data.
hint_l1: Core random number generation primitives and data conversion utilities
hint_l2: An incorrect buffer length check in the random number generation library causes assertions to be too permissive. The size multiplier is applied to the wrong operand in the comparison, allowing destination buffers to not be fully populated. This means a random number generator may be seeded with too little data, weakening the quality of generated random values.

reason: The default value parser uses eval() to execute any string beginning with 'function' as JavaScript code, enabling arbitrary code execution when processing untrusted JSON data.
hint_l1: Data value parsing and input processing pipeline
hint_l2: An arbitrary code execution vulnerability exists where user-controlled string values in the JSON data are automatically interpreted and executed as code without proper sanitization. When displaying or editing data from untrusted sources, crafted input values can trigger execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the browser, leading to full compromise of the client-side application.

reason: The recursive file deletion method accepts file paths without verifying they remain within the intended base directory, allowing directory traversal sequences to delete arbitrary files on the system.
hint_l1: Cache management and temporary file cleanup utilities
hint_l2: A path traversal vulnerability exists in a recursive file deletion routine that does not validate whether target paths remain within an allowed base directory. An attacker can supply specially crafted inputs containing directory traversal sequences to cause the deletion of arbitrary files on the system.

reason: The UnlockNotification type allows creating aliased mutable references from raw pointers in the unlock-notify callback, violating Rust's memory safety guarantees, and drops the mutex guard before signaling the condition variable, creating a data race on shared state.
hint_l1: Concurrency and synchronization primitives for database operations
hint_l2: A data race vulnerability exists in the database unlock notification mechanism where mutable references are unsafely aliased from raw pointers in a concurrent callback context, and a mutex guard is released before a condition variable is signaled, allowing unsynchronized access to shared state.

reason: The virtual table module registration API accepted non-static references to Module structs, allowing them to be freed while SQLite still held raw pointers to the module's function table, creating a data race leading to potential use-after-free.
hint_l1: Database extension and plugin module management
hint_l2: A race condition vulnerability allows memory safety violations because the API for registering database extensions does not enforce that certain data structures remain valid for the required duration. References with insufficient lifetime guarantees can be freed while the underlying C library still holds pointers to them, leading to use-after-free conditions.

reason: The VTab and VTabCursor traits could be safely implemented by any type without enforcing the required #[repr(C)] memory layout with sqlite3_vtab/sqlite3_vtab_cursor as the first field, allowing data races and memory safety violations when SQLite's C code accesses struct fields through the FFI boundary.
hint_l1: Database extension module and plugin interfaces
hint_l2: A data race vulnerability exists where certain trait interfaces for extending the database engine do not enforce required memory layout safety invariants on their implementing types. Implementations are expected to maintain a specific C-compatible memory layout for correct interaction with the underlying C library, but the trait definitions allow any type to provide an implementation. This can result in memory safety violations and data races when the database engine concurrently accesses memory through the FFI boundary at offsets that do not correspond to the actual Rust struct layout.

reason: In the session extension bindings, temporary CString objects created by str_to_cstring() are immediately converted to raw pointers via .as_ptr() in the same expression, causing the CString to be dropped while the pointer is still used in subsequent FFI calls.
hint_l1: Native FFI bindings and database extension API wrappers
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists where a temporary string object is converted to a raw C pointer and immediately deallocated, leaving a dangling pointer that is subsequently passed to a native API function call. This can lead to memory corruption, potentially allowing an attacker to achieve arbitrary code execution.

reason: The capacity growth method deallocates the existing heap-allocated backing storage when called with a size equal to the current capacity, causing a double free and use-after-free on subsequent accesses.
hint_l1: Dynamic array capacity management and memory reallocation logic
hint_l2: A use-after-free and double free vulnerability exists in the capacity growth logic of a dynamically-sized array. When the array is asked to grow its backing storage to a size equal to its current capacity, it incorrectly deallocates the existing memory. This results in a double free immediately and use-after-free on subsequent accesses to the array contents. An attacker who controls the target growth size may exploit this to read sensitive memory contents or achieve remote code execution.

reason: The send-window credit accounting uses unchecked u32 addition when applying WindowUpdate frames from the remote peer. A malicious peer can send a WindowUpdate with a large credit value that overflows the u32 send window, causing an arithmetic panic and crashing the connection.
hint_l1: Stream multiplexing flow control and window management
hint_l2: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the stream multiplexer's send-window accounting. A remote peer can send a specially crafted window update frame with a very large credit value that causes arithmetic overflow in the per-stream window state, triggering a panic in the connection state machine. This results in a remotely exploitable denial of service that does not require authentication.

reason: The length and capacity arguments are swapped when constructing a vector from raw parts during type transmutation, leading to heap memory corruption or out-of-bounds access.
hint_l1: Data type conversion and raw memory manipulation routines
hint_l2: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists due to incorrect ordering of size parameters when constructing a container from raw memory during type conversion operations. This can lead to heap memory corruption or data leakage.

reason: The sandbox's property access handler applies prototype chain checks only in an else branch, allowing function objects to bypass the validation and access dangerous prototype methods like __lookupGetter__, enabling sandbox escape and arbitrary code execution.
hint_l1: Expression evaluation and runtime property resolution
hint_l2: A prototype pollution vulnerability exists in a JavaScript sandboxing library where prototype chain validation during property access is conditionally applied, allowing it to be bypassed for certain object types. An attacker can exploit this gap to obtain references to object prototypes outside the sandbox boundary, which can then be used to construct arbitrary function calls and achieve remote code execution in the host Node.js environment.

reason: A TOCTOU mismatch in property key validation allows attackers to pass objects with custom string coercion that return different values during the safety check versus the actual property access, enabling sandbox escape and arbitrary code execution.
hint_l1: Expression evaluation and property access resolution logic
hint_l2: A sandbox escape vulnerability exists due to a time-of-check time-of-use (TOCTOU) mismatch in property key validation. The sandbox validates property access keys by coercing them to strings during safety checks, but does not enforce that the same string value is used during the actual property lookup. An attacker can supply a specially crafted object whose string coercion returns different values on successive calls, returning a benign key during the security check and a dangerous key during the actual access. This allows bypassing prototype access restrictions and achieving arbitrary code execution outside the sandbox.

reason: The Scalyr Agent before 2.1.10 does not verify the server hostname during SSL/TLS connections, allowing man-in-the-middle attacks even when certificate chain validation is enabled.
hint_l1: Network connectivity and remote server communication
hint_l2: The agent's TLS implementation fails to verify the hostname of the server's SSL certificate in certain code paths. Although the certificate chain is validated against trusted roots, the lack of hostname verification means an attacker who possesses a valid certificate for any domain could intercept and tamper with encrypted communications via a man-in-the-middle attack.

reason: A premature reference count decrement on a Python list object in the error handling path of a C extension function causes a use-after-free, allowing potential access to freed memory.
hint_l1: N-dimensional image processing and labeled array operations
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in a Python C extension where a reference count on a list object is prematurely decremented during error handling, potentially allowing freed memory to be accessed by subsequent operations.

reason: The SliceMoveSource and SliceMoveSourceCollection trait implementations for arrays and boxed slices use ptr::read to duplicate element ownership without inhibiting drops on the source, causing a double free if the user-provided closure panics during unwinding.
hint_l1: Collection element ownership transfer and iteration utilities
hint_l2: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in collection element move operations where elements read via raw pointer duplication are not properly guarded against panic unwinding. If a user-provided closure panics during element consumption, previously moved elements may be dropped twice, leading to undefined behavior including potential memory corruption.

reason: Missing namespace controller that enforces pod security labels on tenant namespaces allows authenticated users to deploy unrestricted workloads and escalate privileges to gain cluster-level control.
